name: Evan Zhao
headline: OpenAI Is Shutting Down Sora – Here's What You Need To Know
hook: It was back in September 2025 when OpenAI launched Sora 2 and it made headlines, seeming to be quite successful. They even secured a $1 billion partnership with Disney. It is quite surprising that OpenAI suddenly decided to shut down Sora. This should be a signal of a strategic pivot. Specifically, reports suggest Sora had a very heavy computation cost of up to $15 million per day, while user engagement was also dropping. It seems that OpenAI is refocusing on its core competitive arena. Sora seemed to have been a “side quest” distracting from the high-stakes race in text and code generation, where rivals like Anthropic are gaining lots of ground.
analysis: Sora consumed massive GPU resources, much more than text or code generation. OpenAI’s most constrained and strategically valuable resource is compute capacity. Allocating that capacity to a low-margin and low-retention consumer video generation service was not appealing. The tradeoff would be that by killing Sora, high cost computation resources would be less constraint for use of other core products like ChatGPT. Moreover, this also promotes engineering focus on these other core products. It is also where many customers rely on these services from OpenAI more for reliability and scale. Companies that are negatively impacted by this decisions would be Disney, whose $1B deal evaporated. Similarly, creators who invested time building workflows around Sora. In terms of five forces, the high supplier power of GPU scarcity made video generation which is compute-intensive very expensive. Moreover, OpenAI allowed buyers to use a flat subscription which was great for the buyers, but terrible for their margins.
question: If OpenAI is no longer focusing on video generation to cut cost, how will this impacts goals on developing a multimodal AI? Also, will there ever be breakthroughs in efficiency to eventually make high-quality video generation viable at scale? Maybe Sora's shutdown is only temporary...

name: Rui Wang
headline: After Apple's internally-built Siri AI failed quality tests (reportedly "failing up to a third of the time"), Apple chose Google's Gemini model to power a completely new version of Siri.
hook: Google pays Apple roughly $20 billion a year for the right to be the default search engine on every iPhone. That arrangement made Apple one of Google's most important distribution partners, and gave Apple a revenue stream that required no engineering. Now Apple is turning to the same company to supply the intelligence layer of a rebuilt Siri. The direction of dependency has shifted: Apple used to collect from Google; now it is paying Google for a capability it could not build itself.
analysis:
1. Supplier Power: Apple and Google have long had a mutually dependent relationship. Google needed iPhone distribution; Apple needed search revenue. The bargaining looked roughly balanced. This deal changes that. Apple now relies on Google for search and for the core intelligence layer of Siri. Google has become something closer to essential infrastructure for the iPhone experience. When a supplier becomes infrastructure, the buyer (Apple)'s exit options shrink, and bargaining power in future negotiations is weakened accordingly. 

2. RBV: Apple's chip design, its OS integration, its manufacturing relationships — these remain genuinely hard to replicate. What this story reveals is that the model layer requires a fundamentally different organizational capability: massive datasets, rapid iteration on open-ended language tasks, comfort with probabilistic output. Apple's culture, perfectionism, secrecy, tight release cycles, may be precisely what made building a frontier LLM difficult. A company's deepest strengths can also define the boundary/limits of what it can build.
question: Apple's differentiation has historically required controlling every layer of the experience. If the intelligence layer is now Google's, what exactly is Apple differentiating on and more importantly, is that enough to justify the premium over Android?
